chapter_title: Type Conversion, Type Aliases, and Type Deduction

cards.front: Can you use auto as a function return type?
cards.back:
Yes. The compiler deduces the return type from the return statements in the function body.
auto add(int x, int y) { return x + y; } // returns int

cards.text: When using auto as a function return type, the compiler deduces the type from the {{c1::return statements}} in the function body.

cards.question: What return type is deduced?
cards.explanation: The return statement returns 5.0 (a double literal), so the compiler deduces the return type as double.

cards.front: What restriction applies when using auto return type with multiple return statements?
cards.back: All return statements must deduce to the SAME type. If different return statements would deduce different types, the code won't compile.

cards.question: Why does this fail to compile?
cards.answer: Compile error: Different return statements deduce different types (int vs double).
cards.explanation: All return statements must deduce to the same type when using auto. Mixing int and double return values causes a compilation error.

cards.text: When using auto return type with multiple return statements, all returns must deduce to the {{c1::same type}}.

cards.front: What is a trailing return type and what is its syntax?
cards.back:
A way to specify the return type AFTER the parameter list using ->:
auto add(int x, int y) -> int { return x + y; }
The auto is just a placeholder; the actual type comes after ->.

cards.text: Trailing return type syntax: auto functionName(params) {{c1::->}} ReturnType { }

cards.front: Should you use auto return types for normal functions in everyday code?
cards.back:
No. Explicit return types are preferred for normal functions because:
  • Better documentation and readability
  • Clearer intent
  • Easier for others to understand the API
  • Better error messages
auto return types are mainly useful for advanced cases like templates.

cards.front: Can you use auto for function parameters in C++20?
cards.back: Yes, but it creates a function template, not a normal function. Each call with different argument types generates a different function instantiation. This is abbreviated function template syntax.

cards.question: What does this function do in C++20?
cards.answer: Creates a function template where x and y can be any types that support operator+. Each call with different types generates a new function.
cards.explanation: This is abbreviated function template syntax (C++20). auto parameters make the function a template, allowing it to work with any types.

cards.concept: When auto return types are useful
cards.explanation:
auto return types are most useful in:
  • Lambda expressions (default behavior)
  • Function templates where return type depends on template parameters
  • Advanced metaprogramming
  • Forwarding functions
For normal functions, explicit return types are clearer and preferred.
examples: // Template where return type depends on operation
auto multiply(T x, U y) { return x * y; }

cards.text: When using auto as a function return type with multiple return statements, all returns must deduce to the {{c1::same type}}.
Trailing return type syntax uses auto as a placeholder: auto func(params) {{c1::->}} ReturnType { }
In C++20, using {{c1::auto}} for function parameters creates a {{c2::function template}}, not a normal function.
